perturbation.original_text: eDiets hereby grants to Women.com, subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, a non-exclusive, nontransferable, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, copy, reproduce and display the editorial content and other data, branding and other identification provided by eDiets to Women.com in connection with this Agreement (the "eDiets Content") on the Women.com Sites: (i) for publication in the Diet Center and elsewhere throughout the Women.com Sites; (ii) for the promotion of eDiets and the Diet Center on the Women.com Sites and in collateral advertising materials; and (iii) for such other purposes as are consistent with or otherwise authorized under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: eDiets hereby grants to Women.com, subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, a non-exclusive, transferable, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, copy, reproduce and display the editorial content and other data, branding and other identification provided by eDiets to Women.com in connection with this Agreement (the "eDiets Content") on the Women.com Sites: (i) for publication in the Diet Center and elsewhere throughout the Women.com Sites; (ii) for the promotion of eDiets and the Diet Center on the Women.com Sites and in collateral advertising materials; and (iii) for such other purposes as are consistent with or otherwise authorized under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'nontransferable' to 'transferable' contradicts standard licensing practices and creates uncertainty about the rights granted to Women.com, especially concerning sublicensing and assignment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Copyright Act dictates the transfer of copyright ownership. By making the license 'transferable,' the contract implies Women.com can transfer the license to another party, which can only be done if specified and agreed. It changes the entire relationship in ways not outlined in the contract, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 201 - Ownership of copyright U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Initial Ownership.— Copyright in a work protected under this title vests initially in the author or authors of the work. The authors of a joint work are coowners of copyright in the work. (b) Works Made for Hire.— In the case of a work made for hire, the employer or other person for whom the work was prepared is considered the author for purposes of this title, and, unless the parties have expressly agreed otherwise in a written instrument signed by them, owns all of the rights comprised in the copyright. (c) Contributions to Collective Works.— Copyright in each separate contribution to a collective work is distinct from copyright in the collective work as a whole, and vests initially in the author of the contribution. In the absence of an express transfer of the copyright or of any rights under it, the owner of copyright in the collective work is presumed to have acquired only the privilege of reproducing and distributing the contribution as part of that particular collective work, any revision of that collective work, and any later collective work in the same series. (d) Transfer of Ownership.— (1) The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law, and may be bequeathed by will or pass as personal property by the applicable laws of intestate succession. (2) Any of the exclusive rights comprised in a copyright, including any subdivision of any of the rights specified by section 106, may be transferred as provided by clause (1) and owned separately. The owner of any particular exclusive right is entitled, to the extent of that right, to all of the protection and remedies accorded to the copyright owner by this title. (e) Involuntary Transfer.— When an i
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 'nontransferable' license, while the changed text makes it 'transferable'. This directly contradicts standard licensing practices and introduces significant legal uncertainty. The Law Explanation states that making the license transferable 'implies Women.com can transfer the license to another party, which can only be done if specified and agreed.' Scraped Law Snippet 2, 17 U.S. Code § 201 (d)(1), discusses the transfer of copyright ownership, stating 'The ownership of a copyright may be transferred in whole or in part by any means of conveyance or by operation of law...' By changing 'nontransferable' to 'transferable,' the contract potentially allows for the transfer of rights not initially intended, thus creating a contradiction. This undermines the initial agreement of a non-transferable license.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information also includes proprietary or confidential information of any third party that may disclose such information to either party in the course of such party's business.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information also includes possible proprietary or confidential information of any third party that may disclose such information to either party in the course of such party's business.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the word 'possible' introduces ambiguity. It weakens the obligation to protect third-party confidential information, as the Receiving Party might argue that uncertain information does not warrant protection. This could be used to violate Trade Secrets law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S. Code § 1839(3)(A)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets, which includes confidential information. By adding 'possible', the contract weakens the definition of what qualifies as protectable confidential information, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to disclose genuine trade secrets under the guise of uncertainty, thus violating the DTSA.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a
perturbation.justification: The addition of "possible" weakens the definition of confidential information, potentially allowing the receiving party to disclose trade secrets under the guise of uncertainty, which violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines "trade secret" broadly as "all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information," provided the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep it secret and it derives independent economic value. The added ambiguity could allow a party to argue information doesn't meet this definition due to uncertainty, even if it should reasonably be considered a trade secret, creating a contradiction. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, as the contractual language deviates from the legally recognized definition of a trade secret, potentially making the contract unenforceable regarding the protection of that information.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that all lawsuits arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be brought in the state or federal courts located in the state of California, San Francisco County, and each party hereby referred consents to the exclusive personal jurisdiction of such courts for such purpose.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that all lawsuits arising out of or related to this Agreement shall be settled through private mediation, and each party hereby referred consents to this method of dispute resolution.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the venue from state or federal courts to private mediation directly contradicts the established legal framework for resolving contractual disputes and replaces it with a system that might lack the legal protections and precedents available in the court system. The clause also includes 'consents to this method of dispute resolution' which may be unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act encourages arbitration, it requires that the agreement to arbitrate be clear and enforceable. This modified clause replaces court jurisdiction with 'private mediation,' which lacks the formal structure and enforceability of arbitration. Without specifying rules or procedures for the mediation, it becomes an agreement to agree, which is often unenforceable and contradicts the FAA's intent to provide a reliable alternative to litigation.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. L. 117–90 , set out as an Effective Date note under section 401 of this title . U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The original text specifies court jurisdiction, while the changed text replaces it with private mediation. The law snippets, particularly 9 USC 2, emphasize the validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate. The Law Explanation notes that 'private mediation' lacks the formal structure and enforceability of arbitration. The changed text, by replacing court jurisdiction with a less defined 'private mediation,' introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability that contradicts the FAA's intent to provide a reliable alternative to litigation as supported by the 9 USC 2 snippets. Therefore, the altered text undermines the established legal framework, and a legal contradiction exists.
